title: Post-intubation hypotension
trigger: New hypotension after RSI, tube placement, or positive-pressure ventilation.
immediateMoves: Confirm pulse, blood pressure, waveform, oxygenation, and tube depth.
Disconnect briefly if severe auto-PEEP/dynamic hyperinflation is plausible.
Reduce excessive PEEP or tidal volume while maintaining oxygenation.
Give IV fluid bolus 500-1000 mL crystalloid if preload-dependent physiology is suspected.
Push-dose epinephrine 5-20 mcg IV every 2-5 minutes: mix 1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL (cardiac) epinephrine into 9 mL saline = 10 mcg/mL; give 0.5-2 mL per dose.
Start norepinephrine early at 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min and titrate to MAP.
Consider tension pneumothorax, mainstem intubation, hemorrhage, sepsis, or peri-intubation arrest physiology.
reassess: Repeat BP every 1–2 minutes until stable
avoid: Do not assume the tube is the only problem.
Do not forget analgesia/sedation after paralysis wears off.
references: Procedures starter clinical review content. Replace with formal reviewer-approved references before release.

title: Failed airway / cannot intubate
trigger: Failed attempts, falling oxygen saturation, poor view, inability to ventilate, or airway contamination.
immediateMoves: Call failed-airway plan out loud and assign roles.
Reoxygenate with two-hand BVM, oral/nasal airway, PEEP valve, and suction.
Change something before the next attempt: operator, blade, position, device, bougie, or approach.
Place supraglottic airway if BVM or intubation is failing and time allows.
Move to front-of-neck access if cannot oxygenate.
avoid: Do not repeat the same failed attempt.
Do not burn the last 30 seconds debating while oxygenation is failing.
references: Procedures starter clinical review content. Replace with formal reviewer-approved references before release.

title: Arterial puncture during central line
trigger: Bright pulsatile blood, arterial waveform, high-pressure tubing, or ultrasound concern during access.
immediateMoves: Stop. Keep wire/catheter controlled; do not dilate if position is uncertain.
Confirm with ultrasound, pressure transduction, blood gas, or waveform if stable enough.
If only small finder/needle puncture, remove and hold firm pressure.
If dilator or large catheter entered artery, leave it in place and call vascular/surgery immediately.
Document event, site, exam, and consultation.
reassess: Airway compression risk for neck sites
avoid: Never pull a large-bore arterial catheter and 'just hold pressure' without the right backup.
references: Procedures starter clinical review content. Replace with formal reviewer-approved references before release.

title: Lost guidewire / wire control problem
trigger: Wire no longer visible or control of the proximal wire is lost.
immediateMoves: Stop the procedure immediately.
Do not advance catheter or dilator blindly.
Obtain imaging to localize the wire if not immediately retrievable.
Call IR/vascular based on local resources.
File safety event and document honestly.
avoid: Do not pretend it did not happen. This is a patient-safety emergency.
references: Procedures starter clinical review content. Replace with formal reviewer-approved references before release.

title: Failed transvenous pacer capture
trigger: Pacer spikes without electrical/mechanical capture, persistent bradycardia, or unstable hypotension.
immediateMoves: Confirm generator is on, rate appropriate, output high, and connections secure.
Increase mA until capture, then set safety margin above threshold.
Slowly advance/withdraw catheter while watching ECG and patient pulse/BP.
Use ultrasound/fluoro/CXR if available to confirm course.

Continue transcutaneous pacing while troubleshooting; if hypotensive, start push-dose epinephrine 5-20 mcg IV every 2-5 minutes (1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL cardiac epinephrine into 9 mL saline = 10 mcg/mL, give 0.5-2 mL per dose) or norepinephrine infusion 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP.
avoid: Do not accept electrical spikes as success without a pulse/BP response.
references: Procedures starter clinical review content. Replace with formal reviewer-approved references before release.

title: Procedural sedation apnea
trigger: Hypoventilation, apnea, desaturation, loss of airway tone, or rising ETCO2 during sedation.
immediateMoves: Stop sedative dosing and call for airway help.
Reposition airway, jaw thrust, suction, and add oral/nasal airway as needed.
BVM with PEEP if ventilation inadequate.

Reversal only when appropriate for agent/context and never instead of ventilation: opioid - naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV, titrate every 2-3 minutes; benzodiazepine - flumazenil 0.2 mg IV over 15 seconds ONLY in benzodiazepine-naive patients (seizure risk).
Prepare for intubation if oxygenation or ventilation cannot be maintained.
avoid: Do not stare at the pulse ox while the patient is not ventilating.
references: Procedures starter clinical review content. Replace with formal reviewer-approved references before release.

title: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST)
trigger: Neurological or cardiac symptoms during or after local anesthetic injection — including seizure, altered mental status, dysrhythmia, or cardiac arrest. May occur immediately or up to 60 minutes after injection.
immediateMoves: Stop local anesthetic injection immediately.
Call for 20% lipid emulsion (Intralipid) now — this is the antidote and must not be delayed.
Secure airway early if seizure or loss of consciousness: high-flow O2, prepare to intubate.
If cardiac arrest: start ACLS; avoid vasopressin; use standard epinephrine doses sparingly (≤1 mcg/kg); begin lipid emulsion simultaneously.
Lipid emulsion 20%: bolus 1.5 mL/kg IV over 1 minute, then infusion 0.25 mL/kg/min; may repeat bolus in refractory arrest (max cumulative dose ~12 mL/kg).
Treat seizure with benzodiazepine (midazolam 1-2 mg IV, or 0.05 mg/kg, repeat as needed); avoid propofol (myocardial depressant and dilute lipid — not a substitute for Intralipid).
reassess: Rhythm and BP every 1-2 minutes until stable
Response to lipid emulsion — cardiac recovery is the therapeutic endpoint
SpO2 and ETCO2 continuously if intubated
avoid: Do not give vasopressin in LAST cardiac arrest (ASRA guideline).
Do not use propofol as a substitute for lipid emulsion — it causes myocardial depression.
Do not give high-dose epinephrine — small doses only (≤1 mcg/kg) if needed.
Do not assume resolution is complete — CNS toxicity may outlast cardiac toxicity; observe ≥4-6 hours.
references: Neal JM et al. ASRA Practice Advisory on Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2018.
Di Gregorio G et al. Clinical presentation of local anesthetic systemic toxicity. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2010.

title: Pneumothorax after central venous catheter
trigger: Respiratory distress, hypoxia, or decreased breath sounds following IJ, subclavian, or axillary central venous catheter placement.
immediateMoves: Assess immediately: vital signs, O2 saturation, breath sounds bilaterally, JVD, tracheal position.
Bedside ultrasound: absent lung sliding ipsilateral to line site confirms pneumothorax.
For tension physiology (hypotension + absent breath sounds + JVD or tracheal deviation): needle decompression immediately — do not wait for imaging.
For large or symptomatic pneumothorax without tension: chest tube or pigtail catheter.
For small, asymptomatic pneumothorax: supplemental O2, serial imaging, close monitoring in a monitored setting.
reassess: Vital signs and SpO2 every 15 minutes
Repeat CXR at 4-6 hours for observed small pneumothorax
Response to chest tube drainage (air leak on water seal, lung re-expansion)
avoid: Do not dismiss absent lung sliding ipsilateral to a subclavian or IJ line as artifact.
Do not delay needle decompression for tension pneumothorax to obtain imaging.
Do not attempt a contralateral central line without first securing the ipsilateral pneumothorax.
Do not clamp a chest tube in a suspected ongoing air leak without expert guidance.
references: Hind D et al. Ultrasonic locating devices for central venous cannulation. BMJ. 2003.
ACEP Clinical Policies on emergency management of pneumothorax.

title: Chest tube malposition
trigger: Poor drainage, persistent air leak, respiratory deterioration, pain out of proportion, or imaging showing tube in fissure, subcutaneous tissue, or abdomen after chest tube or pigtail catheter placement.
immediateMoves: Obtain CXR immediately: confirm tube position, presence of residual pneumothorax or effusion, and diaphragm relationship.
Confirm the drainage circuit is not kinked, clamped, or obstructed before declaring malposition.
Subcutaneous tube (no drainage, crepitus tracking along tube): remove and replace — provides no pleural drainage.
Intrafissural tube (tube in the major or minor fissure): may drain slowly; replace or add a second tube if drainage is inadequate.
Tube traversing the diaphragm into the abdomen: stop drainage immediately; do not pull without surgical consultation — intra-abdominal hemorrhage risk.
reassess: Drainage output and character every 1-2 hours after repositioning
Air leak assessment: fluctuation in water seal chamber with respiration
Repeat CXR after repositioning or replacement
avoid: Do not advance a chest tube after initial placement — introduces a non-sterile segment into the pleural space.
Do not clamp the tube for a suspected large bronchopleural fistula without expert guidance — tension pneumothorax risk.
Do not remove a tube that has crossed the diaphragm without surgical presence and a vascular injury plan.
Do not assume inadequate drainage is tube failure before ruling out circuit obstruction.
references: ATLS Advanced Trauma Life Support guidelines on chest tube management.
Bosman A et al. Complications and malposition of thoracic drains. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg. 2010.

title: Laryngospasm
trigger: Sudden partial or complete glottic closure during or after sedation, airway manipulation, or extubation. Presents as stridor (partial) or silent obstruction with paradoxical chest movement and desaturation (complete).
immediateMoves: Remove all airway stimuli immediately: stop suctioning, airway manipulation, and the precipitating procedure.
100% O2 via tight-fitting BVM mask; jaw thrust and chin lift to optimize airway alignment.
Apply CPAP-level pressure with the BVM (10-20 cmH2O) — sustained positive pressure may break partial laryngospasm.
If not breaking within 30-60 seconds: succinylcholine 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV (subparalyzing dose) to break spasm; prepare for possible intubation.
If complete obstruction and impending arrest: succinylcholine 1-2 mg/kg IV and immediate intubation. Have surgical airway ready.
reassess: Chest rise with each positive-pressure breath — absence indicates complete closure
HR: bradycardia during severe laryngospasm signals impending cardiac arrest
avoid: Do not force a laryngoscope or airway device through a closed glottis — worsens spasm and laryngeal trauma.
Do not use jaw thrust without BVM mask seal — Larson's point maneuver (bilateral firm pressure at the condylar notch posterior to the jaw ramus) may help break spasm without an airway device.
Do not delay succinylcholine if BVM ventilation is failing — early use prevents full cardiorespiratory arrest.
Do not use ketamine for sedation without suction immediately available — increased secretions can precipitate laryngospasm.
references: Gavel G, Walker RWM. Laryngospasm in anaesthesia. Continuing Education in Anaesthesia, Critical Care & Pain. 2014.
Larson CP. Laryngospasm — the best treatment. Anesthesiology. 1998;89:1293-1294.
